beats.text: A single train-test split gives you one accuracy number, but that number is itself a random draw from the data.
The honest target is generalization error, the expected loss over the whole distribution, which you can only estimate from a finite sample.

visual.note: True error: expected loss over the data distribution

beats.text: For a 0-1 loss the holdout accuracy carries a binomial standard error that shrinks only as one over the square root of the test size.

visual.note: Standard error of a holdout accuracy

beats.text: At 85 percent accuracy on 200 test rows the standard error is about 0.025, giving a 95 percent interval near plus or minus five points.
Repeated holdouts of the same data and model wander widely, so two unlucky splits can disagree by several points on luck alone.
A point estimate with no spread is an anecdote, not a result, so cross-validation replaces the single split with repeated resampling.
k-fold cross-validation partitions the data into k equal disjoint folds and validates on each fold exactly once.
The estimate is the average of the k fold scores, with a standard error equal to the fold spread divided by the square root of k.
The choice of k is a bias-variance dial: small k trains on less data and biases pessimistically, while leave-one-out is nearly unbiased but high variance.
The empirical sweet spot, established by Kohavi and unchanged in 2026, is five or ten folds: low bias, manageable variance, affordable compute.
On 100 rows under five-fold CV each validation fold holds 20 rows, leaving the other 80 for training that round.
Total compute is exactly k model fits, and that multiplier is the price you pay for the error bars the single split could not give.
Plain k-fold shuffles rows and cuts blindly, which fails under class imbalance and when rows are not independent.
Stratified k-fold partitions within each class so every fold mirrors the overall label distribution up to rounding.

visual.note: Each fold matches the dataset's class proportions

beats.text: A dataset of 20,000 rows at a 1 percent positive rate holds only 200 positives in total.
Stratified five-fold splits those 200 positives evenly, so each fold carries 40 minority examples and produces a meaningful score.
Grouped k-fold keeps every shared identity entirely on one side of the split, since a sibling row across the boundary leaks the answer.
The two compose into stratified group k-fold, the standard recipe for imbalanced clustered data, with transforms fit inside the training fold only.
For temporally ordered data shuffling is not harmless, because it lets the model train on the future and validate on the past.

visual.sub: The future never trains the past

beats.text: The forward-chaining constraint demands that every training timestamp precede every validation timestamp in every fold.

visual.note: Forward-chaining constraint, for every fold i

beats.text: Standard k-fold violates this on roughly half of its train-validation pairs and is therefore invalid for any series with temporal structure.
An expanding window keeps all history up to the cut and validates on the next block, growing the training set each fold.
A rolling window slides a fixed-length training block forward, dropping the oldest data and adapting to non-stationarity and concept drift.
A further refinement inserts an embargo gap between train and validation to kill leakage from overlapping feature windows or label horizons.
If you tune hyperparameters and report on the same cross-validation folds, the reported number is biased upward.

visual.sub: Select and score on disjoint data

beats.text: Selecting the best of many noisy estimates selects partly for noise, so the winner's score is optimistic by construction.

visual.note: The optimism of selection grows with the grid size

beats.text: Nested CV uses an outer loop only to score and a separate inner loop inside each outer training set to search and refit.
On pure-noise labels the naive best-CV score climbs above 0.5 while the nested outer mean honestly hugs the true 0.5.
The cost is outer times inner times grid model fits, which is why nested CV is reserved for when an honest number is non-negotiable.

visual.note: Total model fits for nested CV

beats.text: Chapter 02 opens the inner loop with grid and random search, Bayesian optimization, and the budget arithmetic behind expensive fits.
